title: Curatorial practice and exhibition rationale
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to developing a sophisticated curatorial practice for the IB Visual Arts HL Exhibition. It focuses on writing a compelling Curatorial Rationale and effective Exhibition Texts, aligning with the top markband descriptors for Criterion A and B. Students will learn how to articulate the conceptual and formal links within their body of work to create a coherent and impactful exhibition.
learningObjectives: Analyse the role of a curator in shaping the meaning and reception of artworks.
Articulate a clear and convincing Curatorial Rationale that justifies the selection, arrangement, and interrelationship of artworks.
Synthesise conceptual ideas with material and technical choices across a body of work.
Write concise and analytical Exhibition Texts that connect individual artworks to the overall curatorial theme.
Evaluate spatial arrangements to guide the viewer's journey and enhance the exhibition's conceptual strength.

simpleExplanation.title: Your Exhibition: From Artist to Curator
simpleExplanation.summary: Your exhibition isn't just a collection of your best pieces; it's a single, unified argument presented visually. The Curatorial Rationale is your thesis statement, explaining the 'why' behind your show. It's your opportunity to guide the examiner and viewer through your artistic investigation, connecting your ideas, materials, and final artworks into a coherent whole.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a film director. Your individual artworks are like scenes in a movie. By themselves, they might be interesting, but they don't tell the full story. As the curator (director), you must edit them together (sequence the artworks), create a compelling narrative (the curatorial theme), and write the director's notes (the Curatorial Rationale) to explain your vision and how each scene contributes to the overall plot and mood of the film.
steps: Identify the central, unifying concept or question that runs through your most resolved artworks. This is your curatorial 'big idea'.
Select 8-11 artworks that best demonstrate the development and exploration of this concept. Consider how they 'speak' to each other.
Draft your Curatorial Rationale (max 700 words), clearly stating your intentions and explaining how your conceptual and material choices create a coherent body of work.
Write a concise Exhibition Text (max 500 characters per piece) for each artwork, linking its specific formal and conceptual qualities back to your main rationale.

flashcards.back: A written statement (max 700 words) that explains the overarching theme, concepts, and intentions of your exhibition. It justifies the selection and arrangement of works, articulating the coherence of the exhibition as a whole.

The clear and convincing interrelationship between the artworks in an exhibition. A coherent body of work demonstrates a sustained and focused investigation of a particular theme or concept, evident through both formal and conceptual links.

The effective merging of ideas, materials, and techniques. In your exhibition, this means demonstrating how your conceptual intentions are successfully realised through your choice of media and application of skills.

A brief text (max 500 characters) accompanying each artwork. It should concisely explain the work's title, medium, size, and offer a brief analytical insight into how it connects to the overall curatorial theme.
A key component of the Curatorial Rationale where you explicitly state what you aimed to explore, investigate, or communicate through your body of work.

The physical placement of artworks within the exhibition space. This is a curatorial decision that influences the viewer's journey, creates relationships between works, and can reinforce the exhibition's conceptual meaning.
The path and experience a viewer has moving through your exhibition. Effective curation guides this journey, creating a narrative flow, points of emphasis, and moments of reflection.
The visual elements of an artwork, such as line, colour, shape, texture, composition, and scale. Your rationale should connect these qualities to your conceptual goals.
The ideas, themes, and intentions behind the artwork. High-level work shows depth and personal engagement with complex concepts, which are clearly articulated in the rationale and texts.
The ability to express your ideas, intentions, and reflections clearly and precisely in written form. This is crucial for the Curatorial Rationale and Exhibition Texts.
How the artworks in your exhibition connect and communicate with one another. This can be through shared subject matter, recurring motifs, a developing visual language, or a progression of ideas.

Avoid simply describing what is in your artwork. Instead, analyse *how* your formal choices (e.g., 'the use of fragmented brushstrokes') create a specific effect or meaning (e.g., 'to convey a sense of psychological fracture').

faq.q: What if my artworks are in very different media? Can they still be coherent?
faq.a: Absolutely. Coherence is not about uniformity. If you use diverse media, your Curatorial Rationale must provide a strong justification. Explain how the choice of different media was a deliberate strategy to explore different facets of your central concept. For example, you might use sculpture to explore physicality and video to explore time, with both media serving a unified investigation into the human body.

faq.q: How personal should my Curatorial Rationale be?
faq.a: It should be personal in that it reflects your unique artistic investigation and intentions ('My aim was...'). However, it should not be a diary entry or an emotional confession. Maintain an academic, analytical tone. Ground your personal vision in the language of art analysis, discussing your conceptual and formal choices rather than just your feelings.

faq.q: Do I need to physically set up my exhibition to photograph it?
faq.a: Yes. The IB requires you to submit photographs of your overall exhibition in situ, showing the arrangement of the works in a physical or virtual space. This is where your understanding of spatial arrangement is assessed. The quality of these photographs matters, as they are the examiner's only view of your complete, curated show.

faq.q: How does the Curatorial Rationale relate to my Process Portfolio?
faq.a: Think of the Process Portfolio as the 'making of' documentary and the Exhibition and Rationale as the final film. Your PP shows the journey, the experiments, the failures, and the thinking process. Your Rationale is a polished, conclusive statement about the final, resolved body of work. While the Rationale is informed by the journey in your PP, it should not simply repeat it. It focuses on the final outcome and its conceptual and formal success.

sections.content: Welcome to the curatorial aspect of your Visual Arts journey. The Exhibition is not merely a display of your technical skills; it is the culmination of your artistic investigation. Your role shifts from solely being an artist to becoming a curator of your own work. This lesson will equip you with the tools to craft a compelling narrative for your exhibition, ensuring your conceptual intentions are communicated with clarity, depth, and sophistication, directly addressing the requirements for the highest markbands in the Exhibition assessment.
1. Understanding Curatorial Practice: Beyond Hanging Pictures

Curatorial practice is the act of conceptualising, selecting, organising, and interpreting artworks for presentation. For your IB Exhibition, this means you must make deliberate choices to construct a specific meaning. You are not just showing 8-11 artworks; you are presenting a single, coherent 'body of work'. The examiner assesses this coherence (Criterion A) and the conceptual strength (Criterion B) largely through the lens of your curatorial decisions and the rationale you provide.

items: Curation is an act of authorship; it shapes the viewer's understanding.
Your selection of works must be justified. Why these pieces, and not others?
The arrangement in space is a key part of the message. Consider sightlines, groupings, and pacing.
Your written texts (Rationale and Exhibition Texts) are the primary tools for articulating your curatorial vision to the examiner.

sections.content: The Curatorial Rationale is the single most important piece of writing in your Exhibition component. It is your direct communication with the examiner, explaining the 'what', 'why', and 'how' of your exhibition. A top-band rationale is not a summary of your Process Portfolio; it is a persuasive, analytical text that makes a case for your exhibition as a resolved and coherent body of work.

items: **Introduction & Statement of Intent:** Begin by clearly stating the central theme or question of your exhibition. What did you set out to investigate?

**Body Paragraphs - Thematic/Conceptual Links:** Explain how the artworks collectively explore this theme. Discuss the interrelationship between pieces. How does one work inform another? How does the body of work show a development or deepening of your ideas?

**Body Paragraphs - Formal & Material Synthesis:** Discuss how your technical choices serve your concepts. Why did you choose specific media? How do your use of colour, composition, or scale reinforce your ideas? This demonstrates the synthesis of form and content.
**Conclusion & Overall Vision:** Summarise the overall impact you want your exhibition to have on the viewer. Reiterate the coherence of the body of work.

sections.question: Sample excerpt from a high-scoring Curatorial Rationale for an exhibition titled 'Constructed Landscapes'.
sections.solution:
My exhibition, 'Constructed Landscapes', investigates the tension between natural environments and human memory. I am not interested in depicting topographically accurate places, but rather in exploring how landscapes are filtered, fragmented, and reconstructed through the subjective lens of recollection. This body of work synthesises digital manipulation and traditional printmaking to question the authenticity of a remembered place.

The decision to combine digital collage with photogravure is central to this conceptual inquiry. In works like 'Estuary I' and 'Estuary III', initial photographic documentation is digitally deconstructed, with elements layered and distorted to mirror the non-linear process of remembering. These digital files are then translated into the physical, tactile medium of photogravure. The rich, velvety blacks and embossed plate marks of the print lend a false sense of historical weight and authenticity to what are, in essence, fabricated images. This deliberate friction between the ephemeral digital source and the permanent, authoritative print is the core formal strategy used to articulate my ideas about the fallibility and subjectivity of memory. The recurring motif of the fractured grid, visible in both the digital compositions and the scratched surfaces of the copper plates, serves as a visual metaphor for memory's structure—a network of interconnected yet incomplete fragments.

sections.content: In your Rationale, use the language of the assessment criteria. Phrases like 'This coherent body of work explores...', 'I synthesised digital and traditional media to...', and 'The conceptual intention behind this choice was...' signal to the examiner that you are consciously meeting the assessment requirements.

Coherence is the holy grail of the IB Exhibition. It is what separates a collection of good artworks from an excellent exhibition. Coherence is achieved when there is a clear, discernible connection running through the works. This connection can be conceptual (a recurring theme), formal (a consistent visual language or colour palette), or material (a sustained investigation of a particular medium). The strongest exhibitions demonstrate coherence across all three areas.

items: **Conceptual Coherence:** Ensure all selected works relate to the central idea in your rationale. Ask: does this piece add to the conversation, or is it an outlier?
**Formal Coherence:** Look for visual threads. This could be a recurring motif, a specific approach to composition, a limited colour palette, or a consistent way of handling materials.

**Material Coherence:** Your choice of media should feel intentional. If you use multiple media, you must justify in your rationale how this diversity serves your conceptual goals (e.g., using both sculpture and photography to explore presence and absence).
**Synthesis:** The highest achievement is when your formal and material choices are inseparable from your conceptual ideas. The 'how' (technique) perfectly serves the 'why' (concept).

sections.content: The Exhibition Text for each piece is a micro-version of your rationale. It must be incredibly concise and focused. Its job is to anchor the individual artwork to the exhibition's main theme. Avoid simply describing the work; instead, provide a brief analytical insight that helps the viewer understand its significance within the larger body of work.

sections.question: Sample Exhibition Text for the artwork 'Estuary III' from the 'Constructed Landscapes' exhibition.
sections.solution:
Title: Estuary III
Medium: Photogravure on Hahnemühle paper
Size: 45cm x 60cm

Text: This print merges digital fragments of a coastal path with an imagined horizon. The deep etching and plate tone lend a deceptive permanence to the digitally manipulated scene, questioning the reliability of memory and our construction of 'place'. The scratched grid disrupts the image, echoing the fractured nature of recollection.

sections.content: The 500-character limit for Exhibition Texts is strict and includes spaces. Write your text in a word processor with a character count function. Be ruthless in your editing. Every word must count. Use analytical verbs: 'questions', 'disrupts', 'conveys', 'explores', 'juxtaposes'.
